---
title: "Data_Wrangling"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## Running Code

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

```{r}
BiocManager::install("remotes")
BiocManager::install("microbiome/mia")
library(BiocManager)

```

```{r}

library(mia)
library(knitr)

data("GlobalPatterns")
tse <- GlobalPatterns

# Agglomerate to phyla
tse <- agglomerateByRank(tse, rank = "Phylum")

# Split data based on sample type
tse_list <- splitOn(tse, by = "samples", group = "SampleType")

# Loop over the list of TreeSEs, and get top features for each
top_taxa <- sapply(tse_list, getTop)

top_taxa |> kable()
```

```{r}
#The information contained by the colData of a TreeSummarizedExperiment can be added and/or modified by accessing the desired variables. You might want to add or modify this data to include new variables or update existing ones, which can be essential for ensuring that all relevant metadata is available for subsequent analyses.
data("GlobalPatterns")
tse <- GlobalPatterns

# Modify the Description entries
colData(tse)$Description <- paste(
    colData(tse)$Description, "modified description"
)

# View modified variable
tse$Description |> head()

```

```{r}
# Working loop ERROR!!!!!!
# New info can be added to the exp by creating a new variable
#new_data <- runif(ncol(tse))

#colData(tse)$NewVariable <- new_data


```

```{r}
# Create a matrix with random values
mat <- rnorm(ncol(tse) * nrow(tse), 0, 1)
mat <- matrix(mat, ncol = ncol(tse), nrow = nrow(tse))

# Add matrix to tse
assay(tse, "random", withDimnames = FALSE) <- mat

assayNames(tse)

tse2 <- cbind(tse, tse)
tse2

```

```{r}
# Merge Data
# Take subset for demonstration
# Take subsets for demonstration purposes
tse1 <- tse[, 1]
tse2 <- tse[, 2]
tse3 <- tse[, 3]
tse4 <- tse[1:nrow(tse),4]

# With inner join, we want to include all shared rows. When using mergeSEs
# function all samples are always preserved.
tse <- mergeSEs(list(tse1, tse2, tse3, tse4), join = "inner")
tse


```

```{r}
library(knitr)

# For several custom analysis and visualization packages, such as those from tidyverse, the SummarizedExperiment data can be converted to a long data.frame format with meltSE().
# Melt SE object
molten_tse <- meltSE(
    tse,
    assay.type = "counts",
    add.row = TRUE,
    add.col = TRUE,
)

molten_tse |>
    head() |>
    kable()
```

```{r}
#For MultiAssayExperiment data, you can instead utilize longForm().
data(HintikkaXOData)
mae <- HintikkaXOData

molten_mae <- longForm(
    mae,
    i = c("counts", "nmr", "signals"),
    colDataCols = c("Rat", "Diet")
)

molten_mae |>
    head() |>
    kable()
```

```{r}
# tidy R programming
data("GlobalPatterns", package = "mia")
tse <- GlobalPatterns

library(tidySingleCellExperiment)

tse 
```

```{r}
tse %>%
    join_features(features = rownames(tse), shape = "long") %>%
    group_by(.cell) %>%
    mutate(total_counts = sum(.abundance_counts)) %>%
    group_by(SampleType) %>%
    summarise(total_counts = mean(total_counts))

library(ggplot2)
BiocManager::install("miaViz")

library(miaViz)

tse %>%
    filter(SampleType == "Soil") %>%
    transformAssay(method = "clr", pseudocount = TRUE) %>%
    join_features(features = rownames(tse), shape = "long") %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = .cell, y = .abundance_clr, fill = .cell)) +
    geom_boxplot()


```

```{r}
#Exercise 
### Defining FILE PATHS ###

url_to_assay <- url("https://github.com/microbiome/data/raw/main/OKeefeDSData/okeefe_assay.csv")
url_to_coldata <- url("https://github.com/microbiome/data/raw/main/OKeefeDSData/okeefe_coldata.csv")
url_to_rowdata <- url("https://github.com/microbiome/data/raw/main/OKeefeDSData/okeefe_rowdata.csv")


### Importing FILES ###

okeefe_assay <- read.csv(url_to_assay, row.names = 1)
colnames(okeefe_assay) <- gsub("\\.", "-", colnames(okeefe_assay))

okeefe_coldata <- read.csv(url_to_coldata, row.names = 1)
okeefe_rowdata <- read.csv(url_to_rowdata, row.names = 1)

okeefe_assay
okeefe_rowdata
okeefe_coldata

tse <- TreeSummarizedExperiment(assays = list(counts = okeefe_assay),
                                colData = okeefe_coldata,
                                rowData = okeefe_rowdata)


colData(tse)$group <- sample(
  c("group1", "group2"),
  size = ncol(tse),
  replace = TRUE
)

plotBarplot(tse, col.var = "group")

as.data.frame(colData(tse)) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = group, fill = group)) +
    geom_bar() +
    labs(title = "Sample distribution by group",
         x = "Group",
         y = "Number of samples")

tse_list <- splitOn(tse, by = "samples", group = "group")
tse_list

lapply(tse_list, function(x) unique(colData(x)$group))

tse_list <- lapply(tse_list, function(x) {# Sum all counts in this TreeSE
    total <- sum(assay(x, "counts"))
    # Add it to all samples in colData
    colData(x)$total_counts <- total
    return(x)
})
lapply(tse_list, function(x) head(colData(x)$total_counts))

tse_combined <- unsplitOn(tse_list)

data("enterotype")
tse2 <- enterotype

tse_merged <- mergeSEs(tse_combined, tse2)

dim(tse_merged)

colData(tse_merged)


## Key concepts to remember
#splitOn() splits into a list of TreeSEs
#lapply() loops over the list and returns a modified list
#assay(x, "counts") retrieves the raw count matrix
#unsplitOn() is the reverse of splitOn()
#mergeSEs() handles datasets with different feature sets, unlike cbind() which requires identical rows
```
